_README: Machine-readable benchmark targets — the single source of truth for
every consumer that asks 'how does this run compare to the benchmark'.
The dashboard diff panel, the audit cap calibrations, and any future
regression-bar test all read this file so they stay calibrated against
Provenance: Deli Chen et al., 'From Copilots to Colleagues:
A Survey of Autonomous Research Agents', 45 pp / NeurIPS-style preprint.
Numbers extracted from the reference PDF on 2026-05-29.

benchmark.title: From Copilots to Colleagues: A Survey of Autonomous Research Agents

tolerance._doc: Per-metric Δ tolerances. A run within ±tolerance of the benchmark counts as 'on target' (green). Outside ±tolerance but on the same side counts as 'close' (amber). Anything else is 'gap' (red).

_doc: Hard thresholds tools/audit_writing.py enforces on the assembled .tex.
Living here (rather than as module-level constants) keeps the
dashboard tolerances and the audit gate calibrated from the same
numbers. Each value is justified inline.
If you tighten or relax a threshold, run the full test suite — the
survey_run_dir fixture exercises every invariant against these
audit_thresholds._citation_density_cap_why: 1.5× the benchmark's 8.4 — leaves headroom for citation-dense sections like Related Work without licensing the 'stuff every related work into the parenthetical' anti-pattern.
audit_thresholds._sentence_citation_cap_why: ≥ 4 cites in one sentence is the hallmark of bare-list citing; require those works to be named in prose instead.
audit_thresholds._annotated_bib_min_ratio_why: Benchmark hits 100%; 80% leaves 20% cushion for entries that legitimately lack _decision_summary (venue boilerplate, anthologies).
audit_thresholds._conclusion_words_why: Benchmark sits at 557. The window 400-700 lets surveys with slightly different breadth hit the re-frame shape without enabling a bullet-list summary.
audit_thresholds._top_sections_why: Benchmark sits at 8. Lower bound 6 forbids a flat (≤5, no narrative arc) layout. Upper bound 12 (was 9) gives broad / historical briefs — many dimensions, eras, or paradigm transitions — room to give each its own section instead of being forced to merge everything back into the same ~8-section skeleton (which made every survey look templated and capped length). Beyond 12 risks a lost-in-TOC sprawl.
audit_thresholds._nesting_why: ≥ 4 sections must carry ≥ 3 subsections to produce the 'introduction → 4 patterns → 17 systems → 6 problems' rhythm the benchmark TOC has.
audit_thresholds._no_aux_table_cap_why: We do NOT cap auxiliary table count. The relevant question is not 'how many tables' but 'is each table load-bearing'; the cross_cutting_matrix invariant enforces the latter by requiring every labelled aux table to be cited from the prose via \ref{}/\autoref{}. A run that ships 10 well-referenced tables passes; a run that ships 3 decorative ones fails. This delegates the count decision to the agent writing the survey, which is where it belongs.
